INTRODUCTION

Introduction to Linear Models

Linear models are a class of models widely used in practice. They have been studied extensively and have roots going back over a hundred years.

1/13
SLIDE 04

Linear Models Fundamentals

Linear models make a prediction using a linear function of the features. For linear models for regression, the output, ŷ, is a linear function of the features. The coefficients (w) and the intercept (b) are used to make predictions.

📄 Page 59-70
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-10. Comparing predictions made by nearest neighbors regression for different — Page 58
2/13
SLIDE 05

Binning, Discretization, and Linear Models

Binning, discretization, linear models, and trees are used to represent data. Linear models can only model linear relationships, which are lines in the case of a single feature. Decision trees can build a more complex model of the data.

📄 Page 234
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-1. Comparing linear regression and a decision tree on the wave dataset — Page 235
3/13
SLIDE 06

Univariate Nonlinear Transformations

Univariate nonlinear transformations can help linear models for regression. Adding squared or cubed features can improve performance. Other transformations, such as polynomial features, can also be used.

📄 Page 246-248
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-7: In[34]: — Page 247
4/13
SLIDE 07

Kernelized Support Vector Machines

Kernelized support vector machines can be used to separate classes in a dataset. By adding a third feature derived from the first feature, it is possible to separate the two classes using a linear model.

📄 Page 109
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-29. A two-dimensional dataset in which the feature on the y-axis has a nonmo‐ — Page 93
5/13
SLIDE 08

Univariate Statistics

Univariate statistics can be used to select features for a model. Binning, polynomials, and interactions can have a huge influence on how models perform on a given dataset. This is particularly true for less complex models like linear models and naive Bayes models.

📄 Page 250
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-8. Histogram of feature values for X[0] after logarithmic transformation — Page 249
6/13
SLIDE 09

Interactions and Polynomials

Interactions and polynomials can be used to improve the performance of linear models. By adding polynomial features, it is possible to learn a more complex relationship between the features and the target variable.

📄 Page 238-242
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-3. Linear regression using binned features and a single global slope — Page 239
7/13
SLIDE 10

Summary and Outlook

Linear models are a widely used class of models. They have been studied extensively and have roots going back over a hundred years. Representing data in a way that is suitable for the machine learning model is crucial.

📄 Page 264
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-18. Predictions made by linear regression using a product of the day of week — Page 263
8/13
SLIDE 11

Theory

Many machine learning models use principles from computer science. Implementing a classifier or regressor works by inheriting from a base class and overriding certain methods.

📄 Page 375
9/13
SLIDE 12

Model-Based Feature Selection

Model-based feature selection can be used to select the most important features for a model. Linear models have coefficients, which can be used to capture feature importances.

📄 Page 253
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-19. Coefficients of the linear regression model using a product of hour and day — Page 264
10/13
SLIDE 13

The Danger of Overfitting

Overfitting can occur when a model is too complex and fits the training data too closely. This can be avoided by using techniques such as cross-validation and regularization.

📄 Page 276
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 6-1. Data usage when preprocessing outside the cross-validation loop — Page 321
11/13
SLIDE 14

Decision Trees

Decision trees can be used to model complex relationships between features and targets. They work by recursively partitioning the data into smaller subsets.

📄 Page 96
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-22. A decision tree to distinguish among several animals — Page 85
12/13
✦

Summary

Linear models are a widely used class of models. They have been studied extensively and have roots going back over a hundred years.

Linear models
13/13
